beats.narration: A laminated composite is a stack of plies — and nearly every defect lies flat between those plies, perfectly oriented for a straight beam.
The big three: delaminations, plies separated by bad cure or impact; porosity, fields of tiny voids; and foreign material, film cured into the stack.
A delamination is a resin-to-air interface: a strong echo at its depth, and the back wall goes dark — nothing behind it gets examined.

payload.heading: The delamination signature
payload.note: STRONG ECHO AT DEPTH · BACK WALL GOES DARK

beats.narration: Automated squirters raster the part into a C-scan: delamination, a sharp-edged dead zone; porosity, mottled loss; a film inclusion, its discrete footprint.

payload.heading: Every defect signs the C-scan
payload.note: SHARP EDGE · MOTTLED LOSS · DISCRETE SHAPE

beats.narration: So here's the rule: built and inspected in layers — let the interfaces tell the story.

payload.sub: Let the interfaces tell the story.
